otype_desc: Extremely hot, blue, massive. Rarest type — short lives of only a few million years.
otype_why: O-type stars produce most of a galaxy's ultraviolet light and ionize surrounding gas, creating the glowing emission nebulae we see. Their supernovae seed the universe with heavy elements like iron and gold.
btype_desc: Blue-white giants. Often found in young OB associations and spiral arms.
btype_why: B-type stars trace the spiral arms of galaxies because they are short-lived. Astronomers use them as markers for galactic structure and recent star formation.
atype_desc: White stars with strong hydrogen absorption lines. Many are binary systems.
atype_why: A-type stars like Sirius were among the first to have their spectra analyzed, helping astronomers develop the stellar classification system we use today.
ftype_desc: Yellow-white. Transition zone where convection begins in the outer layer.
ftype_why: F-type stars are interesting for exoplanet searches because they have habitable zones and lifespans long enough for complex life to potentially develop.
gtype_desc: Our Sun is a G2V star! Yellow stars with lifespans of ~10 billion years.
gtype_why: G-type stars like our Sun prove that modest stars can nurture life. Their 10-billion-year lifespan gives plenty of time for biological evolution.
ktype_desc: Orange stars. Many have habitable zones — prime candidates for exoplanet searches.
ktype_why: K-type stars are considered the best candidates for finding habitable exoplanets—they are stable, long-lived, and common enough to offer many opportunities.
mtype_desc: Red dwarfs — 76% of all stars! Extremely long-lived (trillions of years).
mtype_why: M-type red dwarfs will be the last stars shining in the universe. Proxima Centauri b, a potentially habitable exoplanet, orbits one of these stars—our closest neighbor!
orion_nebula_desc: Stellar nursery 1,344 light-years away. Visible to the naked eye. Contains the Trapezium star cluster.
eagle_nebula_desc: Home of the Pillars of Creation. Star-forming region 7,000 light-years from Earth.
crab_nebula_desc: Supernova remnant from 1054 AD. Contains a pulsar spinning 30x per second.
carina_nebula_desc: One of the largest nebulae. Contains Eta Carinae, a hypergiant 4 million times brighter than the Sun.
helix_nebula_desc: The "Eye of God." A planetary nebula — the outer shell of a dying Sun-like star.
ring_nebula_desc: Classic planetary nebula in Lyra. The central white dwarf is visible at high zoom.
horsehead_nebula_desc: Dark nebula silhouetted against the emission nebula IC 434. An iconic astronomical object.
lagoon_nebula_desc: One of the brightest emission nebulae. Visible with binoculars in Sagittarius.
barred_spiral_desc: Like our Milky Way. A central bar of stars with spiral arms winding outward. ~60% of spirals have bars.
grand_design_spiral_desc: Prominent, well-defined spiral arms. Usually triggered by gravitational interaction with a companion galaxy.
elliptical_desc: Smooth, featureless ellipsoidal shape. Contain old, red stars with little gas or dust. Formed from galaxy mergers.
irregular_desc: No distinct shape. Rich in gas and dust with active star formation. Often satellites of larger galaxies.
observe_visible_desc: Human-eye color shows stars, dust shadows, and the overall shape of the galaxy.
observe_visible_note: Best for classifying galaxy shape and comparing star colors.
observe_infrared_desc: Infrared light passes through dusty lanes and reveals warm star-forming regions.
observe_infrared_note: Great for seeing through dust that blocks visible light.
observe_radio_desc: Radio maps trace cold hydrogen gas that outlines spiral arms and future star birth.
observe_radio_note: Hydrogen at 21 cm is one of the best maps of hidden galactic gas.
observe_xray_desc: X-rays highlight the hottest, most energetic regions near compact objects and young massive stars.
observe_xray_note: Useful for black-hole accretion, neutron stars, and supernova remnants.
observe_gravity_desc: A gravity view shows the invisible mass halo inferred from star motions.
observe_gravity_note: This is evidence-based, not a photograph: motion reveals the dark matter halo.
rst_m31_bridge: Naked-eye smudge in dark skies; huge in binoculars.
rst_m31_story: Use this to compare the simulated Milky Way disk with a real neighboring spiral galaxy.
rst_m31_lesson1: Classify the shape: bulge, disk, spiral arms, and dust lanes.
rst_m31_lesson2: Switch to infrared and look for dust-hidden structure.
rst_m31_lesson3: Ask what evidence shows this galaxy is not inside the Milky Way.
rst_m51_bridge: A telescope/photography classic; spiral arms are easier in images than eyepieces.
rst_m51_story: A dramatic case where interaction with a companion sharpens spiral structure.
rst_m51_lesson1: Trace the spiral arms and find the companion galaxy.
rst_m51_lesson2: Compare the bridge of material with the Galaxy Explorer interaction model.
rst_m51_lesson3: Predict where star formation is strongest before changing surveys.
rst_m87_bridge: Home of the first imaged black-hole shadow, M87*.
rst_m87_story: A smooth elliptical galaxy that anchors the Virgo Cluster and hosts a supermassive black hole.
rst_m87_lesson1: Compare its smooth light to spiral galaxies.
rst_m87_lesson2: Use the catalog overlay to notice the crowded Virgo Cluster field.
rst_m87_lesson3: Connect the bright core to black-hole evidence, not a visible event horizon.
rst_m104_bridge: A bright galaxy where dust lanes make structure visible.
rst_m104_story: A striking real example of how dust can reveal a galaxy disk in silhouette.
rst_m104_lesson1: Find the dark lane and infer the disk orientation.
rst_m104_lesson2: Compare visible and infrared: what changes when dust is less opaque?
rst_m104_lesson3: Decide whether this looks more spiral-like or elliptical-like.
rst_m82_bridge: Best understood with multiwavelength views of gas and dust.
rst_m82_story: A galaxy-wide starburst: intense star formation is driving material out of the disk.
rst_m82_lesson1: Look for the disturbed shape instead of neat spiral arms.
rst_m82_lesson2: Ask how a nearby galaxy interaction could trigger star birth.
rst_m82_lesson3: Use infrared to hunt for dusty star-forming regions.
rst_m1_bridge: A real supernova remnant from the 1054 event.
rst_m1_story: The afterglow of stellar death: a pulsar powers a tangled nebula of expanding debris.
rst_m1_lesson1: Connect the filaments to expanding supernova ejecta.
rst_m1_lesson2: Compare this remnant with Star Life supernova and neutron-star stages.
rst_m1_lesson3: Ask what data would reveal the hidden pulsar.
rst_m42_bridge: One of the best beginner telescope targets.
rst_m42_story: A nearby star-forming cloud where hot young stars light the gas around them.
rst_m42_lesson1: Find the bright core and surrounding gas wings.
rst_m42_lesson2: Compare optical and infrared views to see through dust.
rst_m42_lesson3: Ask why this is a star nursery rather than a galaxy.
rst_pleiades_bridge: Gorgeous in binoculars; too wide for many telescopes.
rst_pleiades_story: A young cluster showing how stars are born together and drift apart over time.
rst_pleiades_lesson1: Count bright blue stars and infer a young age.
rst_pleiades_lesson2: Notice why binoculars can be better than high magnification.
rst_pleiades_lesson3: Ask how an open cluster differs from a globular cluster.
rst_carina_bridge: A southern-sky showpiece made famous by Hubble and JWST imagery.
rst_carina_story: A huge star-forming complex where massive stars sculpt dust pillars and glowing gas.
rst_carina_lesson1: Look for bright cavities carved by young massive stars.
rst_carina_lesson2: Switch surveys and compare where dust is visible versus transparent.
rst_carina_lesson3: Ask why massive stars reshape their birth clouds so quickly.
rst_m16_bridge: The Hubble/JWST Pillars are a famous close-up inside this star-forming region.
rst_m16_story: Dense columns of gas and dust are being eroded by newborn stars while new stars form inside them.
rst_m16_lesson1: Search for dark columns and bright ionized edges.
rst_m16_lesson2: Predict which wavelengths reveal embedded protostars.
rst_m16_lesson3: Compare pillar erosion to stellar feedback in Star Life.
rst_stephan_bridge: A JWST showcase for colliding galaxies, shock fronts, and tidal debris.
rst_stephan_story: A small patch of sky where multiple galaxies are gravitationally disturbing one another.
rst_stephan_lesson1: Identify which galaxies look distorted by interaction.
rst_stephan_lesson2: Ask what a shock front would look like in non-visible wavelengths.
rst_stephan_lesson3: Compare the group to M51: one companion versus several galaxies.
rst_cartwheel_bridge: A JWST/Hubble-friendly example of a collision-generated ring wave.
rst_cartwheel_story: A smaller galaxy likely punched through the disk, sending a star-forming ring outward.
rst_cartwheel_lesson1: Find the ring and compare it to ordinary spiral arms.
rst_cartwheel_lesson2: Ask why collisions can trigger new stars instead of only destroying structure.
rst_cartwheel_lesson3: Use the ring as evidence of a past encounter.
survey_optical_desc: Visible-light plates reveal star color, dust lanes, and galaxy structure.
survey_near_infrared_desc: Infrared light cuts through dust and highlights cooler stars.
survey_mid_infrared_desc: Warm dust and star-forming regions become easier to spot.
catalog_simbad_desc: Scientific object IDs around the target.
catalog_clean_survey_desc: Image-only mode for careful visual inspection.
orion_arm_us_desc: Our Solar System is here, about 26,000 light-years from the center.
perseus_arm_desc: The next spiral arm outward from us. Contains many young, hot stars.
quiz_sun_type: What type of star is our Sun?
quiz_mw_center: What is at the center of the Milky Way?
quiz_hottest_type: Which star type is the hottest?
quiz_our_arm: Which spiral arm contains our Solar System?
quiz_mtype_pct: What percentage of stars are M-type red dwarfs?
quiz_star_count: How many stars are in the Milky Way?
quiz_mw_type: What type of galaxy is the Milky Way?
quiz_closest_star: Which star is closest to our Sun?
quiz_hottest_color: What color are the hottest stars?
quiz_mw_width: How wide is the Milky Way?
quiz_light_cross: How long does it take light to cross the Milky Way?
quiz_sun_fate: What will our Sun become at the end of its life?
quiz_after_red_giant: What stage comes after a Red Giant for a massive star?
quiz_solar_mass_life: How long does a star with 1 solar mass live?
quiz_final_fate: What determines a star's final fate?
cosmic_dawn_desc: The first stars ignite, ending the cosmic dark ages. These massive Population III stars forge the first heavy elements.
first_galaxies_desc: Protogalaxies begin to coalesce from dark matter halos. The first quasars blaze to life, powered by supermassive black holes.
galaxy_assembly_desc: Galaxies collide and merge, building larger structures. Spiral arms begin to form as gas settles into rotating disks.
milky_way_forms_desc: Our galaxy takes shape. The galactic bar forms, organizing the inner structure. Star formation peaks in the spiral arms.
sun_is_born_desc: A cloud of gas collapses in the Orion Arm, forming our Sun and Solar System 4.6 billion years ago. Life will eventually arise on Earth.
mature_galaxy_desc: The Milky Way settles into its current form with 200-400 billion stars. Star formation slows as gas reserves deplete.
present_era_desc: We are here! Humanity looks outward. The universe continues expanding, and dark energy accelerates its growth.
right_now_desc: The observable universe is 93 billion light-years across. We can see the cosmic microwave background—the afterglow of the Big Bang.
stage_brown_dwarf_desc: Too small for sustained hydrogen fusion; it glows faintly while cooling.
stage_cooling_brown_dwarf_desc: A substellar ember fading slowly over cosmic time.
stage_red_dwarf_desc: Burns slowly for hundreds of billions of years.
stage_blue_dwarf_desc: Theoretical phase where a red dwarf heats up as its opacity changes.
stage_black_dwarf_desc: Theoretical future: a white dwarf cooled after far longer than the universe has existed.
stage_ms_desc_sunlike: Hydrogen fusion ignites! Stable for billions of years.
stage_ms_desc_massive: Hot and enormous. Burns through fuel in millions of years.
stage_red_supergiant_desc: Expands to massive proportions, large enough to swallow Jupiter!
stage_supernova_desc_core: Core collapses! A catastrophic explosion outshining entire galaxies.
stage_ms_desc_ultramassive: An ultra-hot blue giant blazing with intense radiation.
stage_blue_supergiant_desc: Sheds immense mass through violent stellar winds.
stage_supernova_desc_hyper: A hypernova explosion obliterates the star.
inspect_galaxytype_evidence: Astronomers classify galaxies by wide-field images, color, gas content, and the motion of stars and dust.
inspect_galaxytype_question: Which visible features helped you classify this galaxy?
inspect_blackhole_desc: The Milky Way contains Sagittarius A*, a compact object of about four million solar masses at the galactic center.
inspect_blackhole_fact2: Bright accretion disk when gas falls in
inspect_blackhole_fact3: Nearby stars orbit at extreme speeds
inspect_blackhole_evidence: The strongest evidence is motion: stars whip around an unseen, tiny, massive object in the core.
inspect_blackhole_question: What evidence would convince you the mass is compact instead of spread out?
inspect_spiralarms_desc: Spiral arms are traffic jams of stars, gas, and dust. Stars move through them while gas compresses and forms new stars.
inspect_spiralarms_evidence: Blue star clusters, emission nebulae, and radio maps of hydrogen reveal where arms are strongest.
inspect_spiralarms_question: Why do blue stars mark recent star formation better than red stars?
inspect_gasclouds_desc: Cold hydrogen and glowing ionized gas are the raw materials for new stars and nebulae.
inspect_gasclouds_evidence: Hydrogen emission and radio wavelengths show gas that visible-light images can miss.
inspect_gasclouds_question: Where would you look for the next generation of stars?
inspect_dustlanes_desc: Dust is not empty darkness. Tiny grains absorb visible light and help cool gas clouds so stars can form.
inspect_dustlanes_evidence: Compare visible and infrared views: dust hides stars in one wavelength and glows in another.
inspect_dustlanes_question: Why can infrared telescopes see deeper through dusty regions?
inspect_darkmatter_desc: Outer stars orbit too fast for visible matter alone. A large invisible halo must be adding gravity.
inspect_darkmatter_evidence: Rotation curves stay flat instead of falling, showing extra unseen mass around the galaxy.
inspect_darkmatter_question: Why is motion better evidence here than a photograph?
inspect_star_evidence: A spectrum reveals temperature, composition, motion, and class from the pattern of absorption lines.
inspect_star_question: How would this star change the galaxy if many formed at once?
inspect_nebula_evidence: Color, emission lines, shape, and nearby stars tell whether this is a nursery, remnant, or dying-star shell.
inspect_nebula_question: Is this object making stars, showing a dead star, or blocking light?
mission_step_inspect_nebula: Inspect a nebula or gas cloud
hubble_tuning_fork_title: Hubble Tuning Fork — classified by shape
aria_hubble_tuning_fork: Hubble tuning fork: ellipticals on the handle, spirals on the top prong, barred spirals on the bottom prong, with the current galaxy type highlighted
hubble_scheme_note: Edwin Hubble's 1936 scheme sorts galaxies by SHAPE — it is NOT a timeline. Galaxies do not evolve along the fork from one type to the next.
aria_two_spectra: Two spectra: a nearby galaxy's absorption lines, and a distant galaxy's same lines shifted toward the red end (redshift).
redshift_note: Each dark line is the fingerprint of the same element. In a more distant galaxy those lines sit farther toward red — expanding space stretched the light on its way here (Hubble's law: recession speed ∝ distance).
doppler_lab_title: Doppler Shift Lab — toward = blue, away = red
doppler_lab_desc: Move the source along your line of sight. Negative radial velocity moves spectral lines toward blue; positive radial velocity moves them toward red.
aria_doppler_spectrum: Doppler shift spectrum: absorption lines move left for blueshift and right for redshift.
aria_doppler_velocity: Doppler radial velocity in kilometers per second
doppler_blueshift_body: the source is moving toward us, so wave crests arrive closer together and wavelengths get shorter.
doppler_redshift_body: the source is moving away, so wave crests arrive farther apart and wavelengths get longer.
myth_model: This 3-D view is a MODEL — no spacecraft has ever photographed the Milky Way from outside. We live inside the disk, which is why we see it edge-on as a band of light across the night sky.
myth_not_solar_system: A galaxy is NOT a solar system. Our entire solar system is just one of ~100–400 billion star systems in the Milky Way.
myth_constellation: Stars in a constellation only LOOK close together — they're often wildly different distances away, just along the same line of sight.
myth_expansion: Cosmic expansion stretches SPACE ITSELF — galaxies aren't flying outward through space, and there's no center. The Big Bang happened everywhere at once, not at one spot.
webgl_error_desc: WebGL failed to initialize. Your browser or device might not support 3D hardware acceleration.
aria_galaxy_canvas: Galaxy simulation — use arrow keys to orbit, +/- to zoom, R to reset view
rotation_intro: Pick a rotation model and watch the stars in the 3-D view above actually obey it. This one question — “how fast do outer stars orbit?” — led to one of the biggest discoveries in physics.
rotbtn_rigid_hint: toy model — spins like a DVD
aria_rotation_curve: Rotation curve chart: orbital speed versus distance from the galactic center. The Keplerian prediction from visible matter falls off with distance, but the observed curve stays flat. The shaded gap between them is the evidence for dark matter.
rotation_explain_keplerian: If starlight were all there is, gravity weakens with distance and outer stars should crawl — watch the galaxy's center above lap the outskirts and the disk shear apart. Our solar system really works this way: Mercury laps Neptune 700 times per Neptune-year.
rotation_explain_rigid: A toy model — the whole disk turns together like a painted DVD. No real galaxy does this; it would need mass to keep growing with radius squared. Compare it with the other two models!
rotation_explain_flat: In the 1970s Vera Rubin measured real galaxies and found this: outer stars move just as fast as inner ones. Visible matter can't supply that much gravity — an invisible halo of dark matter (~85% of all matter in the universe) must be holding the galaxy together. Nobody yet knows what it is.
rotation_density_wave_note: Also notice: the glowing gas lanes hold still while stars stream through them — real spiral arms are density waves (cosmic traffic jams), not fixed pinwheels of stars.
aria_trigger_supernova: Trigger a random supernova in the galaxy view
title_trigger_supernova: Trigger a random supernova flash in the galaxy view
supernova_unavailable: Supernova effect is not available yet.
warp_blackhole_desc: Sagittarius A* sits in this crowded core; stars orbit it so quickly that an unseen compact mass is required.
realsky_loading: Loading Aladin Lite, sky survey tiles, and catalog services.
card_observe_body: Move this target into Night Sky & Astronomy to compare real survey data with telescope expectations.
card_time_body: Jump to Universe with the matching real-data tour, evidence thread, and guided mission selected.
card_analyze_body: Use Data Lab for the next step: spectra, brightness, color, classification, and student research questions.
aria_blackhole_canvas: Interactive model of a rotating black hole with an event horizon, photon ring, accretion disk, polar jets, and a tidal-forces object-drop experiment.
blackhole_drag_hint: Drag to orbit - Scroll to zoom
blackhole_keyboard_help: Keyboard controls: use the arrow keys to orbit, plus and minus to zoom, and Home to reset the camera. Animation can be paused with the button after the canvas.
blackhole_description: A teaching model near a rotating black hole. Distances are visual, not to scale.
bh_status_reduced_motion: Animation paused to honor your reduced-motion preference.
bh_spin_desc: Higher spin speeds the inner disk and strengthens its bright approaching side.
tidal_forces_desc: Release an object and observe spaghettification: gravity pulls harder on its near side, stretching it radially while compressing it sideways.
two_views_title: Two views of time and light
bh_observer_view_desc: Illustrative observer view: the traveler experiences their own clock normally, while a distant observer receives increasingly delayed and redshifted light signals.
bh_mass_note_stellar: Stronger tidal gradient: disruption begins farther outside the horizon.
bh_mass_note_supermassive: Gentler at the horizon: a compact object can cross before extreme stretching develops.
